description: Canonical terminology registry for Hollow House Institute governance standards.

terms.definition: The principle that humans retain final decision authority and ethical accountability in AI-mediated systems.
A non-bypassable mechanism allowing a human operator to halt system execution.
A defined threshold where automated system output requires human review or intervention.
A governance architecture where human oversight is integrated into system decision processes.
Controls governing how AI systems operate during real-time execution.
The structural layer responsible for enforcing system rules and policies.
The hierarchical architecture of governance standards and enforcement mechanisms.
A structured set of rules limiting system behaviors.
Runtime restrictions preventing unsafe or unauthorized system actions.
Controlled pathways governing how information flows through systems.
The canonical source defining governance terminology.
The requirement that systems reference canonical definitions rather than redefining terms locally.
Preservation of stable term meanings across documentation and systems.
Gradual alteration of term meanings across contexts or system implementations.
Ongoing verification that systems remain aligned with governance requirements.
Documented evidence supporting governance verification.
Traceable record of system decisions and governance interventions.
Compiled documentation demonstrating compliance and governance verification.
Independent confirmation that governance mechanisms operate correctly.
A system structured to allow immediate compliance evaluation.
Operational limits within which a system remains compliant with governance requirements.
A governance condition where monitoring or intervention mechanisms fail.
The condition in which system operations remain trustworthy and unaltered.
The ability of governance systems to remain effective during operational stress or change.
The ability of a system to maintain functional operation during disruption.
Long-term behavioral patterns that stabilize into operational system structures.
Observable data describing patterns of human-AI interaction over time.
Gradual deviation from expected behavioral patterns in AI-human interaction systems.
Mechanisms regulating the influence between humans and AI systems.
Risks emerging from repeated interaction between humans and AI systems.
The cycle of input, system output, and human response shaping system behavior over time.
The condition where system outputs remain aligned with defined governance principles.
The total exposure of a system to governance-related failures.
Risk that operational behavior diverges from defined governance constraints.
Observable indicators that system behavior remains governed and consistent.
A workflow where AI agents perform actions with conditional autonomy.
An AI system architecture where governance authority remains internally controlled.
The delegation of cognitive tasks from humans to AI systems.
Increasing reliance on AI guidance during decision-making processes.
The spectrum between full human control and full AI autonomy.
Preservation of independent human judgment in AI-mediated environments.
